title: Identifying chicken lice and mites
subtitle: How to find red mite, northern fowl mite, and lice; where to look; what treatment involves
excerpt: External parasites are common in backyard flocks and easy to miss until numbers are high. This guide shows you where to look on the bird and in the coop, how to tell the three main species apart, and what management steps each one requires.
sourceNotes: Gail Damerow, The Chicken Health Handbook, 2nd ed. (2015), chapter on external parasites. APHA Poultry health note: red mite in laying hens (UK, 2022). RSPCA welfare guidance on poultry parasites.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dermanyssus gallinae, a blood-feeding mite that lives in coop cracks and joins during the day and feeds on birds at night. It is grey or white when unfed and bright red after a blood meal. One of the most economically damaging poultry parasites in Europe.

Ornithonyssus sylviarum, a mite that lives permanently on the bird, unlike red mite which retreats to the coop structure. Colonies concentrate around the vent area and cause feather damage, anaemia, and skin irritation.

A chewing louse (order Phthiraptera) that feeds on feather debris and skin scale rather than blood. Several species affect chickens. Heavy infestations cause restlessness, feather damage, and weight loss. Unlike mites, lice spend their entire life cycle on the bird.

content.text: A flock with external parasites often shows no obvious signs until the infestation is heavy. Monthly checks on the birds and the coop catch problems early. The three species to know are
red mite
northern fowl mite
, and
poultry louse
. Each lives in a different place and needs a different response.
Red mite: where and when to look

Red mite does not live on the bird in daylight. It hides in cracks, joints, under perch ends, and in nest box corners. Check for red mite in the coop structure, not on the bird. Run a white piece of tissue along the underside of a perch. Dark red or brown smears mean mites are present. In a heavy infestation you will see moving grey specks when you disturb the hiding sites.

Red mite numbers peak in warm months from May to September but can survive year-round in an insulated coop. Birds with red mite often show pale combs from anaemia, stop using nest boxes, and may lose condition despite eating well. Egg production drops.

attrs.title: The night check
attrs.body: Check for red mite at night by torch. The mites are active after dark and you will see them moving on the bird and on the perch surface. In daylight they retreat completely into cracks and a perch-wipe is your only reliable indicator.

content.text: Northern fowl mite: where and when to look

Northern fowl mite lives on the bird at all times. Check the vent area first. Part the feathers around the vent and look for dark crusting, feather discolouration, or small fast-moving specks. The colony concentrates around the vent, abdomen, and base of the tail. Unlike red mite, northern fowl mite is visible during a daytime inspection of the bird.

An infested bird is visibly uncomfortable. She will preen excessively, scratch, and may show feather loss around the vent. Skin under heavy infestations becomes rough and thickened. Northern fowl mite numbers build quickly; a bird that appears mildly infested in one check can be heavily infested two weeks later.
Poultry lice: where and when to look

Lice are flat, fast-moving, and straw-coloured. They are larger than mites and visible to the naked eye. Check under the wings, around the vent, and at the base of the neck feathers. Louse eggs are white clusters attached to the feather shaft close to the skin. These egg clusters (called nits) stay on the feather long after the adults are gone and are a reliable sign of current or past infestation.
How to check a bird
Hold the bird firmly with one hand under the body. Tuck the wings against her sides.
Part the feathers at the vent area with your other hand. Look at the skin.
Check under both wings. Look at the bare skin and the base of the feather shafts.
Part feathers at the neck base. Lice often concentrate here.
Look at the comb and face for any crusting or abnormality.
What treatment means

For red mite, treatment is mainly about the coop structure. Strip the coop, wash all surfaces with hot water, and apply a licensed product to all cracks and joints. A dust bath tray with diatomaceous earth or a licensed powder gives birds a way to self-treat between your coop interventions. Treat the coop every two to three weeks in summer during an active infestation.

For northern fowl mite and lice, treatment goes on the bird. Approved products in the UK include licensed poultry sprays and spot-on treatments. Check product labels for meat and egg withdrawal periods. Treat all birds in the flock at the same time. A second treatment 10 to 14 days later catches eggs that hatched after the first application.

A well-maintained dust bath tray available to the flock year-round reduces lice load naturally. Birds use dust bathing to control feather parasites and will use a tray when ground access is restricted in winter.

entries.cue: Pale combs and dropping egg numbers but no mites visible in daytime coop check
entries.cause: Red mite hides during daylight; a daytime coop check misses an active infestation
entries.fix: Do a night check by torch, or run the white tissue along the underside of the perch. If the tissue smears dark red or you see moving specks after dark, red mite is confirmed.

entries.cue: Treated birds re-infested within a week
entries.cause: Eggs were not killed by the first treatment; re-infestation came from the coop structure or from wild birds
entries.fix: Repeat the on-bird treatment after 10 to 14 days to catch hatching eggs. Check the coop roof and ventilation gaps for wild bird entry points. Wild sparrows and starlings are a common red mite source.

entries.cue: Bird scratches constantly but no parasites found
entries.cause: Dry skin, a dietary issue, or early-stage infestation below visible threshold
entries.fix: Check again in a week. If behaviour persists and no parasites are found, consider a vet check for fungal skin issues or nutritional deficiency.
